summary: DHH pushes back against the 'act your wage' movement, arguing that deliberately underperforming at work hurts the employee more than the employer. He acknowledges that companies often fail to earn their employees' best effort, but contends that withholding effort causes personal skills to atrophy over time. His core argument is that people should always bring their best not as a gift to their employer, but as an investment in their own capabilities and future opportunities.
key_points: Unused abilities atrophy — you can't bank talent or energy for later, only lose it
The 'act your wage' movement is an understandable response to employer mistreatment, but it's ultimately self-defeating
Peak performance should be treated as a personal discipline, not a reward for good employers
Discontent should fuel self-improvement and eventual departure, not complacency
money_quotes: Abilities unused will atrophy, so putting in anything less than your best means giving up on what you're capable of.
You can't save talent or energy for better days, only watch it go to waste.
You can't avenge your grievances by squandering your potential.
If you wait for the perfect company to reveal your peak potential, it may never come.
Whatever the situation, whatever the company, you should show up with your very best. Not for them, but for you.
Use the discontent to become better, then bolt to another camp when the opportunity presents itself.
Don't play yourself by living down to their expectations.
key_insight: Deliberately underperforming to spite a bad employer is a form of self-sabotage — skills atrophy without use, and the only person who loses is you.
